All right, moving right along. Let's talk about sequential submission. So if you go to Cleveland Clinic or Mayo, one of these things for the gigantic executive physical, either a full day or two day and it's five grand, 10 grand, 15 grand, the first thing of course is you got to fill out a crap load of forms. The second thing they do is they take away your clothes. All your clothes are locked in a locker and you're in a little hospital gown and slippers for the rest of the day. So should you decide this is unpleasant and want to go somewhere, good luck. Because you don't have your clothes. And since I would have bailed out about noon, I thought that was a pretty smart thing that they did on their part. It's a little abrupt, but it is a pretty smart thing on their part. The only smart car dealership I've ever been to, to buy a car, takes the car, your car. So usually they send the guy out to look at it and come back in and tell him how much they'll give you for it, right? They take it, it disappears, your car is gone. And they are going to analyze it and decide what it's worth and they're going to wash it and wax it. And you're going to get it back to drive home, look a nice, but it's gone. You are not going anywhere. Test. Bar the doors. You are not going anywhere without a fight until the next break. So there's all sorts of environments where people are somewhat trapped. They usually get there in steps. And the steps continue after the first trap. And the subsequent steps are easier to get somebody through once one of those has happened. I mean, when you've given up your clothes, the rest of the steps, you're not going to object too much. If you let them take your car and make it disappear, the rest of the steps you are not going to object to too much. So the two things to understand about how people kind of move through a process. One is that they always prefer to do it submitting sequentially rather than in one big giant commitment. But at a point fairly early, a significant commitment now makes all the subsequent ones easier. There's a thing about sequential submission or sequential acceptance that very few people in marketing figure out and operate by whenever they create presentations. And that is what does somebody have to agree to sequentially in order to get to the final agreement? So if you think about fire alarms, the final agreement is, I want alarms in my house. Put them in. Right? That's sort of where you got to get them to eventually. And then you're going to deal with price. And can they afford $8,000 worth of alarms? And if they can't, all right, let's omit the basement and we do six. So that's where you are. But how do you get them? And what is all the stuff they have to agree to as they move toward that point that often the marketer or the salesperson would take for granted? So for example, they have to agree that fire, home fires are a significant threat in people's lives. Well, if you're in a fire alarm business or you're a fireman, you pretty much assume everybody agrees with that. It's fact. Everybody knows it. This is not the case. If they don't know anybody whose house is ever caught on fire, they've never had an experience with a house fire. They do not necessarily believe it is a significant threat. And a most significant threat or an urgent threat. Or if they live in a brick house, they may say to themselves, well, I'm not at risk. Brick doesn't burn. Of course, it's the insights yet, but I live in a brick house, right? I don't smoke. They have in their heads, home fires start from people smoking. I don't smoke. We don't know what they think, but we can't be sure that they accept that as a fact. So a good thing to make for yourself in whatever business you're in or whatever thing you're trying to influence people about is your list of sequential agreements that they have to accept to get to the agreement. So there is one, I'm going to show it to you again, but it's in the Opportunity Concepts product. So this is for opportunities, but it applies. A lot of things can be opportunities. And it is 11 agreements. So it's as good an example of any as how this works. So the first agreement is possibility. That this thing we're talking about, whatever it is, that you can lose weight and keep it off. That you can make a lot of money in real estate with no credit and no money. That you can whatever. That it's even possible. Then they have to agree that there is a beneficial nature to it. So yeah, I know you can buy a lot of real estate with no money down and bad credit, but it's really not all that beneficial to me because more people lose money than make money and the tenants steal all the toilets. So they have to agree that there's beneficial nature to the possibility. Then the next thing they have to agree to is that it's attainable. Now that's different than possible. So possible is, yeah, somebody wins the lottery. It's possible. Right? It's possible that you so there's a famous story that of the guy. Can't remember the company now, but it's one of the two jewelry chains that Berkshire Hathaway owns, that Buffett bought. The guy that owns the company bought in bumped into Buffett waiting to cross the street in Manhattan at a traffic light. And they started to talk and he sold his company to Buffett. Well, it's possible. In fact, we know it's possible. It happened. How attainable is it? Where exactly are you going to go hang out? Which street corner are you going to pick and wait until Buffett comes along that you can spring your company on him? Right? So they have to agree that it's attainable. Next, we get to personal possibility. So if I agree it does happen and if I agree that it could be beneficial and if I agree that it happens enough that it could be attainable. Now, is it possible for me? Here we start to get sticking, by the way, with most people. Because most people are pretty easy about the first three. But when we get to it being personal, they are quick to disqualify themselves one way or another from just about everything. My business is different. I'm in a small town, not a big city. I'm in a big city, not a small town. I'm too young. I'm too old. I'm too dumb. I'm too broke. I'm too smart. I'm too... So they quickly begin to disqualify themselves from whatever it is that you are putting in front of them. If you get past personal possibility, again, could it happen to you? Yeah, okay, grudgingly, I guess it could happen to me. But is it practically attainable by you? Is there some process by which it might logically be made to happen to you? Then we get to... We have to... We have to... Because almost everybody about everything and anything has a history of failure at it. At least 95% of people. Right? So everybody is buying a diet. For the most part, there are very few diet virgins. There are some, obviously, because there has to be. But for the most part, the people we're going to sell a diet to have already bought diets before. And they have a track record of failure and disappointment with them. The person we are selling an opportunity to has a track record and a history of failure and disappointment with prior opportunity. The person we are selling a dental coaching program to probably has a history of disappointment and failure with other dental coaching programs. The person we...on and on and on and on, right? Because most people don't use what they buy. So first of all, they have a failure record because they don't use it. And then second of all, most people have the persistence of a nut. So... And by the way, all of us are that way with at least some things. So like, I am famous for buying... It drives Carl and that. So I am famous for buying some kind of gadget. And I mean, at the first irritation, it is in the trash in the garage. I am not, you know, screwing with this. This picture...three pictures on the box. This is how it's supposed to happen. The instruction thing is like this. In the trash. I would have to be something I really wanted. You know, my joke for John for years was if you ship me a kettlebell, which is his deal, you better send somebody with it to lift it. Because I will never get it out of the box. And that will be the end of that. So we have to give him reasons why that history with whatever this is that we are proposing to him is not his fault. So this whole back story he's got, this narrative of, well, I tried that 15 times. We have to fix that narrative by letting him off the hook from it. Tied to that, we have to give him reasons why this time this is different. Almost every customer for almost everything is essentially Charlie Brown lining up to go kick the football. Lucy is holding. That's what they are. Because that's been their life experience. And somehow we got to convince him, Lucy gets away with it in the comic strip by just saying, no Charlie Brown, this time it will be different. We usually have to do a little more work than that. But we have to convince him that this time this will be different for some reason. So I have a client in the get rich in real estate business. I'll show you some of this stuff tomorrow because it's instructive for a different reason than I'm going to talk about right now. But we are almost 50% of the time we are selling to people who have bought a lot of get rich in real estate stuff. We are using other get rich in real estate gurus lists. And so I'm real sensitive to this. There's got to be reasons why this time this thing is going to be different than all your prior experience. And we created four. So I have a four reason argument why this is different than any other time you've attempted to do anything with this. And if you miss that step, you will usually sort of stop your persuasion right there. Now we finally get to talk about personal beneficial nature. So understand it's easy for somebody to agree that something is generally beneficial. And yet feel it is not personally appropriate for or beneficial to them. So in the men's cosmetic business, which is not a very big business generally, but in that category is the hair transplant business. So is there anybody here in the business been in the business? No, but okay. So well, what you got a like Trump orange thing going on, what do you you're a long way away from needing, you know, hair. Well, I do see it's puffed up. Yeah. Just wait. Eventually, there'll be nothing to puff up. So the but the hair transplant business. So I so I've had a number of clients in that business. Do you know what their number one sales problem is? The reason why people stop short of actually doing it? Right. It's not that they don't believe it works. It's not that they don't believe generally it's beneficial to have a full head of hair, etc. etc. You know what their number one objection is when they back off and finally don't close. I've been married a long time. She isn't going anywhere. I just gave it to you verbatim. And that's this. He got all the way through seven. But at eight personal beneficial nature stopped. Anybody in the business will tell you that's that's what they deal with. We've been buried a long time. She ain't going anywhere. I guess I'll just let it run its course. So if they don't fix that. The sale stops right there. Number nine, advantageous or essential timing. So if we get all the way through that stuff, now we have to get some agreement about why it's important to do it now, not later. Gets us to our buy now customer, our buy later customer. So it has to be a situation that gets worse if we wait. It has to be financial loss if we wait. It has to be somehow it has to be disadvantageous to wait and advantageous to do it now. And so that's the next agreement we got to get. Final next, we move to personal advantageous. So it is possible to generally agree that it is advantageous to begin an exercise regimen when you're 40 instead of waiting until you're 50. That may not be that hard to get everybody to generally agree to. But that doesn't mean that the 42 year old person you are selling to is willing to agree that it is personally important to him. And then last, personal appropriateness. Is this okay? It's a big one with money stuff, by the way, right here. Because you can get them to agree to all this, but if they are really uncomfortable, say with the idea of being rich or getting rich or having a high income, they'll hang up right there. You'll get them all the way through this and then they'll back themselves off because they start to think, well, if I really do a lot better than everybody else, I'll lose all my friends, people won't like me. I don't really deserve this. Dad never made more than 20. Crap now surfaces and stops the sale. So when you're selling increase income increase well, if you aren't dealing with mental at with attitudes about all that, you will probably get derailed with some people at some point, just because of that. I didn't bring it, but we have what I just wrote for Jeff to the docs. There's language about now, I want to talk to you about money. And now there's like five paragraphs of I want you to be rich, you deserve to be rich. There's all this head trip stuff about money, because otherwise I get to a personal appropriateness problem. My mother in law for many years would get in. So she said the same thing every time she got in my car. Oh, this is too good for me. Every time she got in the car. This is too good for me. Now she verbalized it. Imagine how many people don't verbalize it, but like they think it. Glent Turner used to, of course, a note. It was not the problem with fur that there is now. So the Costco and there would be great days. One of the things they all tried to do was get all the women to wear minc coats. So when you went to a big rally meeting, by the way, everybody was in suits that looked just like his, which they sold. And all the women were in minc coats and these big beehive hair days. The film is hilarious, by the way, of that era. But most women would say, oh, I don't really want a minc coat. Where would I wear it? What would people think of me if I had a minc coat? But Glent always said, but it's very dangerous if you're the spouse to get them to try it on. But this stuff is in the way, and if we don't deal with it, the sales process stops. 57, maybe my favorite. There is a direct ratio between the time they'll invest with you and the subsequent likelihood of them buying whatever you're selling, buying into whatever ideas you're selling, the price they're willing to pay for it, and even the happiness they have for it after they buy. So somebody asked about buyer's remorse, I think yesterday, using all this stuff. The faster the sale, the higher the buyer's remorse. The more time they gave you and spent in the process, even the happier they are with what they finally buy. So when you hijack their time, you get everything else. So the wildest one I ever saw, and when I first saw it, I thought, wow, this is some brass ball stuff. And this is pretty cool. So first time I saw Vicki's uncle do an opportunity meeting, I didn't know him. I mean, I just went to see it. So they were selling a $5,000 or so get rich business opportunity thing. Strangers in a meeting room and a holiday. Get off the street. And here's the process. At the end of the three hour meeting, still not knowing really what the hell the business even was. You signed up for, I think it was 900 bucks to the next day show up at four o'clock, get on an airplane, you're going to Nassau. For the three day meeting at which all of this is going to be explained. I sat in the back of the room, I said to my father, Jesus Christ, we're having trouble getting them to our meeting. He's putting them on planes and they don't even know what the hell they're going to. This can't possibly work. I mean, next day, Garrett, Akron Canton Airport right here. Charter flight, everybody's got their bags, they're all getting on the plane, going to Nassau. Where they were locked up for three days, sequential submission, locked up for three days and sold into this opportunity. You know what the close rate was? 100%. Of course, if you got them on a plane, you got them. They're trapped in Nassau for three days and you own the plane. How good you got to be? Don't got to be that good. I was amazed. Years later when I met them, I said, how in the hell did you? He said, really, I worked it backwards. I really just wanted to do something where we had 100% close rate. Okay. Fine. Now, the current trends are, of course, to go in the opposite direction of this. So my premise, I spare no expense, is one thing equals four things. Time obtained affects their likelihood of purchase, the elasticity in the price, what they'll pay. Their happiness with the purchase and therefore their referrals. So this one thing affects four things. Now, anybody, so Greg Luther's in the room, Bill, you've done it. Anybody who's ever done free previews of any kind where people come to a free preview seminar and at that free preview seminar, they buy something. Anybody who's ever done it can tell you the person who came from six blocks away is least likely to buy, least certain to buy. The person who drove the farthest is a lock. Anybody who's ever done localized previews can tell you the same thing. It doesn't matter. Great unwashed masses, doctors, salespeople, don't make any difference. It's a lock. As soon as that guy tells you he came to your free preview at LAX and he flew there from San Francisco, you know he's buying. Period. The guy whose office is diagonally across the street, going to be the hardest guy in the room to close. It doesn't have a damn thing to do with what you do or who they are. It purely has to do with this. That guy, he's got to go get on a plane and go home. He's got another two hours in the opposite direction. Empty handed failed. Greg will also tell you, shipping a horse from one track to another to race, it's ugly. It's like you packing up your whole office and moving it. So going to race at county fairs where you had to go to get your license, but I was getting my license. This is horrible. You pack everything up, you got to leave early because there's not enough stalls. Sometimes you're working out of the field with the horse tied to the trailer. All that's awful. You know what's ten times worse? Doing it reverse going home, having lost. That's miserable. So the guy who gave you a lot of time, a lot of time, who is now empty handed, that's miserable. The guy who gave you a little time who's empty handed, not very painful. So this affects all these things. It affects the likelihood of purchase. There's absolutely no doubt. It affects price. You can charge more if you get more time from them leading up to the purchase. So let's say you're going to buy blinds for your house. If you go to blinds.com and you pick out some blinds and pay for them and have blinds shipped to you. Or you have someone come to your house and run around and measure everything and show you a sample book or iPad presentation with six billion kind of blinds and help you pick out the perfect blinds for that room. Which place do you think you'll spend more money? Of course the person who came to your house. But not for the reason most people think. The reason is purely this. It's this. The price stretched has the time stretched. Happiness with the purchase. Well, the more time you took to make it, the more you build up your anticipation for how happy you were going to be. Now you're happy. And if you're happy, you refer more. So this one thing affects all four things. Jeff and Mike can tell you about the dream room at the mattress store. Virtually 100% of the people who come sleep in the dream room buy something. And they buy a premium price something. Don't think about what they did. They came. They picked out a thing. They got put in the room for them. And they went in. Someone put on their jammies. And they slept in the dream room for an hour. Well, of course. But it doesn't have anything to do with is that mattress any better than the mattress they would have laid on for 15 minutes in the store where you lay on one after another. It has to do with the time they put in. Any cookware guy from the cookware sales era, Ziggs era, who sold pots and pans in the home. Anybody ever do it? Anybody buy that way? Okay. They were all taught. Doesn't mean everybody did it. Fire alarm guys taught the same thing. They're all taught. The first thing you do is you unpack all 36 pieces of the stainless steel cookware. All on the floor or all on the table. First thing you do. Why? Because it takes about a half an hour to pack that shit back up. That's why. If you've ever unpacked a bunch of stuff, by the way, don't go back in the box as easy as it came out of the box. It takes time, which means you can keep talking. So after they've said no for the 16th time, get out of here, you still got to pack it up. And you get to keep talking. More time increases the likelihood of purchase. The really good cookware guys, they cooked dinner. You invited a couple of your friends. They moved in with the crap and they cooked dinner. Close rate, real high. Variety reasons, main one, time. So today everybody is pushing you to do the opposite. I just saw this in Entrepreneur Magazine. It's always a great place now to get crappy advice. Pay attention to how your materials look when scanned quickly as opposed to deliberately considered because no one has the time or inclination to do that anymore. So we know everybody is in a hurry. So how about the same advice to heart surgeons? Don't do anything deliberately. Just assume stuff. Move along as quickly as you can. Cut, paste, because no one has the time or the inclination to go through a long operation anymore. How about to an airline pilot? Screw those checklists. Everybody is in a hurry to get out of here. Load them on the plane, fire that baby up and run it down the runway as fast as you can. Well, we would never do that. Why not? Well, because what they do is important. Oh, oh, oh. So this is bad advice if you're doing something important, but it's good advice if you're doing something unimportant. Two suggestions. Don't do unimportant shit. Do something important. Secondly, do it to somebody who has agreed it's important. Because then, even though they are generally inclined not to pay any attention to anything anymore, they can be specifically inclined to pay a lot of attention to it. So people will say, well, nobody will watch a long video anymore. Like the infomercial has to be dead. Really? One of the biggest current trends is...
